questions.question: What is the evidence grade for exercise as a treatment for PTSD?

options.text: STRONG — well-established as primary treatment
Limited RCT evidence; specialist guidance is required before using exercise for PTSD
PROVEN to cure PTSD
Exercise worsens PTSD in all cases

questions.explanation: PTSD has very limited RCT evidence for exercise as a primary treatment. The research brief explicitly notes that PTSD with significant somatic symptoms requires specialist guidance, as interoceptive awareness (noticing body sensations during exercise) can trigger trauma responses. Exercise may be helpful as part of trauma-informed care, but it's not established as a standalone treatment.

questions.question: Why can interoceptive awareness during exercise be problematic for people with PTSD?

options.text: It can't — interoceptive awareness is always helpful
Noticing body sensations (elevated heart rate, breathing changes, muscle tension) during exercise can trigger trauma responses or flashbacks in people with PTSD
Interoceptive awareness only matters for athletes
PTSD has no connection to body awareness

questions.explanation: Interoceptive awareness means noticing internal body sensations. For people with PTSD, increased heart rate, rapid breathing, or muscle tension during exercise can feel similar to the physiological arousal experienced during trauma, potentially triggering flashbacks or panic. This is why trauma-informed movement approaches emphasize gentle, grounded, and controllable exercise with professional guidance.

questions.question: What type of movement approach is recommended for people with PTSD?

options.text: High-intensity, pushing through discomfort
Trauma-informed movement: gentle, body-awareness-focused, grounded, with options for control and modification — ideally guided by a trauma-informed professional
Competitive sports only
No movement whatsoever

questions.explanation: Trauma-informed movement prioritizes safety, predictability, and control. This includes gentle practices like trauma-sensitive yoga, grounded walking, or guided breathwork — always with the option to modify or stop. The emphasis is on reconnecting with the body in a safe way, not pushing through discomfort. Ideally, this should be done with a trauma-informed movement specialist or therapist.

questions.question: When should someone with PTSD seek specialist guidance before starting an exercise program?

options.text: Never — self-directed exercise is always fine
If they have significant somatic symptoms, trauma triggers related to body sensations, or a history of flashbacks or dissociation during physical activity
Only if they've never exercised before
PTSD doesn't require any special considerations for exercise

questions.explanation: Specialist guidance (trauma-informed therapist, trauma-sensitive yoga instructor, or mental health professional) is essential when PTSD involves significant somatic symptoms, body-related triggers, dissociation, or flashbacks. Exercise can be part of PTSD recovery, but it needs to be introduced carefully and with professional support to avoid retraumatization.

questions.question: What is the key safety principle for exercise and PTSD?

options.text: Push harder to overcome the trauma
Exercise is contraindicated for all PTSD cases
Emphasize safety, control, predictability, and grounding; avoid triggering sensations; work with a trauma-informed professional
Only medication works for PTSD

questions.explanation: The key principle is trauma-informed care: prioritize safety, give the person control over pace and intensity, make movements predictable (no surprises), and emphasize grounding (connection to present moment, not dissociation). Exercise should NEVER feel retraumatizing. If body sensations trigger distress, scale back or work with a specialist.
